coredata.dc:title: Highly-sensitive ratiometric determination of tetracyclines and smartphone-assisted visual assay by carrot-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots-europium ion dual-emission fluorescence probe
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zhaofeng
Xu, Hui
Wang, Lihua
Zheng, Xinxiang
Zhao, Tengzhi
Qu, Bowen
Wang, Hua
coredata.dc:description: The persistent residues of tetracyclines (TCs), particularly tetracycline (TC) and oxytetracycline (OTC) in aquaculture have attracted widespread attention due to their significant threat to the environment and human health. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop facile and highly sensitive methods to detect TC and OTC. In this study, using biomass carrot as the carbon source, polyethyleneimine as the nitrogen source, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (W-CDs) were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method. Subsequently, a ratiometric fluorescent probe was constructed by combining W-CDs with europium ions (Eu3+), enabling the specific recognition of TC and OTC. Based on the opposite response signals, W-CDs/Eu3+ enables the ratiometric detection of TC in a wide linear range of 0.0067–25 μM and OTC from 0.0067 to 21 μM, with a sensitive detection limit down to 2.96 nM and 2.85 nM, respectively. Moreover, under 365 nm ultraviolet (UV) lamp, the color change from blue to red is clearly observed. Furthermore, the smartphone-assisted sensing platform enables both visual and quantitative detection of TC. The method was successfully applied to detect TC and OTC in actual water samples, demonstrating the promising potential of this probe.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Carrot
Ratiometric
Tetracycline
Oxytetracycline
Smartphone-assisted

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of TC and OTC based on W-CDs/Eu3+
The feasibility of the W-CDs/Eu3+ ratiometric FL probe for TC/OTC monitoring
Ratiometric FL monitoring for TC and OTC
Selectivity of the W-CDs/Eu3+ hybrid FL probe for TCs monitoring
Response mechanism of W-CDs/Eu3+ for TC and OTC detection
Smartphone-assisted W-CDs/Eu3+ sensor for TC visualization detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of W-CDs (the upper left corner is a HRTEM image with lattice fringes). (B) size distribution of W-CDs. (C) XPS survey spectra of W-CDs. (D) C1 s XPS. (E) N1 s XPS. (F) O1 s XPS.

FL response spectra of W-CDs/Eu3+ probe in the presence of different concentrations of TC (A) or OTC (B). Linear relationship of F616/F467 versus the concentration of TC (C) or OTC (D). (Inset: FL images of the probe upon incubation with TC/OTC under 365 nm UV lamp.).
The ratios of F616/F467 for W-CDs/Eu3+ probe in the presence of different TCs or various substances.
The preparation approach of W-CDs/Eu3+ ratiometric FL probe and its sensing principle for TC and OTC detection.
Determination of TC in real samples using smartphone-assisted W-CDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL probe.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

head.ce:title: Highly-sensitive ratiometric determination of tetracyclines and smartphone-assisted visual assay by carrot-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots-europium ion dual-emission fluorescence probe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Huzhou University, Zhejiang 313000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences, Huzhou University, Zhejiang 313000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Intelligent Manufacturing, Huzhou College, Huzhou 313000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Intelligent Manufacturing, Huzhou College, Huzhou 313000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Materiobiology, College of Science, Shanghai University Shanghai 200444, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Materiobiology, College of Science, Shanghai University Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The persistent residues of tetracyclines (TCs), particularly tetracycline (TC) and oxytetracycline (OTC) in aquaculture have attracted widespread attention due to their significant threat to the environment and human health. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop facile and highly sensitive methods to detect TC and OTC. In this study, using biomass carrot as the carbon source, polyethyleneimine as the nitrogen source, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (W-CDs) were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method. Subsequently, a ratiometric fluorescent probe was constructed by combining W-CDs with europium ions (Eu3+), enabling the specific recognition of TC and OTC. Based on the opposite response signals, W-CDs/Eu3+ enables the ratiometric detection of TC in a wide linear range of 0.0067–25 μM and OTC from 0.0067 to 21 μM, with a sensitive detection limit down to 2.96 nM and 2.85 nM, respectively. Moreover, under 365 nm ultraviolet (UV) lamp, the color change from blue to red is clearly observed. Furthermore, the smartphone-assisted sensing platform enables both visual and quantitative detection of TC. The method was successfully applied to detect TC and OTC in actual water samples, demonstrating the promising potential of this probe.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are a class of chemical substances that can interfere with the physiological functions of biological cells [1]. They are primarily employed to treat bacterial infections and are widely utilized across various fields [2,3]. Among then, TCs are broad-spectrum antimicrobial antibiotics, which are widely used in aquaculture and animal husbandry due to their low cost, low toxicity, and better oral absorption [4–6]. Unfortunately, long-term use or excessive intake results in the accumulation of residues in environmental water and food, which can cause liver damage in consumers and pose significant risks to human health [6–8]. Therefore, detecting TCs residues in aquatic environments is of critical importance. Currently, numerous traditional analytical methods have been employed for TCs detection, including chemiluminescence (CE) [9], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10], electrochemical analysis [11], capillary electrophoresis [12], and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [13]. Despite their high sensitivity, these methods are hindered by time-consuming procedures and complex operations, limiting their practical applications. Therefore, developing rapid, sensitive and highly selective methods for monitoring TCs are urgently needed.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles, which was discovered in 2004 [14]. Recent decades have witnessed the rapid development of CDs in sensing field due to their excellent fluorescence (FL) stability, biocompatibility, low toxicity and cost-effectiveness. Moreover, the lanthanides have found extensive applications in biomedicine and functional materials owing to their unique optical properties, including long FL lifetimes, large Stokes shifts, optical stability and sharp emission bands [15–18]. Common lanthanide elements include europium (Eu3+) [19–21], terbium (Tb3+) [22,23], and lanthanum [24]. CDs exhibit red FL emission in the presence of Eu3+ upon the addition of target TCs. The β-diketonate configuration in TCs strongly coordinates with europium ions (Eu3+) to form a stable europium-tetracycline complex (Eu3+-TC), which transfers absorbed energy to Eu3+, enhancing its FL emission through a phenomenon known as the “antenna effect” (AE) [25,26]. The combination of Eu3+ and CDs as FL probes have garnered significant attention for rapid detection. For instance, Eu-CDs were synthesized from tannic acid and Eu3+ to detect metanil yellow in turmeric powder by Abdel-Lateef et al [27]. Guo et al. developed a ratiometric FL method for detecting Fe3+ ions by embedding CDs into an Eu3+-doped metal–organic framework [19]. Zhou et al. developed a dual-ratiometric optical probe for the detection of diarsenic acid (DPA) based on europium-doped carbon dots (Eu-CDs) by the ligand displacement strategy [28]. CDs are regarded as ideal ancillary ligands for binding with Eu3+, which can efficiently eliminate the FL quenching of Eu3+ by H2O molecules. Furthermore, the characteristic FL of CDs can be used as a reference signal to achieve ratiometric detection of TCs. Dual-emission ratiometric detection enhances accuracy by eliminating interference from instrumental and environmental factors, outperforming single-signal FL probes. However, most ratiometric probes based on Eu3+ and CDs require specific chemical precursors such as citric acid [15,29–33], uric acid [32] and L-tryptophan [34]. Developing CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent probe from biomass to monitor TCs is both simple and cost-effective [16]. However, there are only a few reports on the biomass-derived Eu3+-CDs system for monitoring antibiotics [16,35,36] and further improvements in sensitivity are still required.

Traditional FL detection devices are often bulky, expensive and operationally complex. In contrast, smartphones, which are nearly ubiquitous, eliminate the need for costly equipment and enable testing at any time and location. The integration of smartphones and related App software can realize real-time and point of care test.

In this work, as illustrated in Scheme 1 , carrot-based nitrogen-doped carbon dots (W-CDs) were synthesized using water as the solvent. By integrating W-CDs with Eu3+ (W-CDs/Eu3+), a dual-response FL probe for TC and OTC detection was established. Upon the addition of TC and OTC, the internal filtering effect (IFE) and static quenching result in a decrease in the blue FL of W-CDs. Simultaneously, energy transfer (ET) from TC/OTC to Eu3+ occurs due to the AE between TC/OTC and Eu3+, enhancing red FL emission. Thus, a ratiometric FL detection method for TC and OTC was achieved, accompanied by a distinct smartphone-assisted visual assay based on FL color changes.

Carrot was purchased from local vegetable market (Huzhou, China). Tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), OTC, chlortetracycline hydrochloride (CTC), gentamycin sulfate (GM), kanamycin sulfate (KAN), enrofloxacin (ENR), ciprofloxacin (CIP), lomefloxacin hydrochloride (LOM), levofloxacin (LVX), azithromycin (AZI), thiamphenicol (TAP), chloramphenicol (CPL), doxycycline hyclate (DOX), Eu (NO3)3·6H2O and polyethyleneimine (PEI) (MW = 800) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Amoxicillin (AMC), streptomycin (SM), clarithromycin (CLR) and erythromycin (EM) were purchased from Macklin Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Other solvents and reagents were of analytical grade and were used without additional purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ⋅cm) was used throughout the whole experiments.

F-7100 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan) was applied to obtain FL spectra. ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were measured on a UH-5700 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) analysis was examined through Thermo Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific., USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was accomplished by JEM-2100F (JEOL, Japan). Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra measurements were performed using Perkin-Elmer spectrnm3 (PerkinElmer, USA). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was recorded on a D8 advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). The FL lifetime decay curves were recorded on FLS 1000 (Edinburgh, Europe). High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) analysis was performed on a Shimadzu LC-16 HPLC equipped with a Shim-pack GWS C18 column (5 μm, 250 × 4.6 mm). Quantitative filter paper was purchased from Hangzhou Special Paper Co and Color Grab APP was used as a FL reading tool in smartphones.

W-CDs were prepared by using carrot as the carbon source, polyethyleneimine (PEI) as the nitrogen source, and water as the solvent. 2 g of chopped carrot powders and 2.55 mL PEI were added to 20 mL of ultrapure water. After the mixture was stirred for 20 min, it was transferred to a 50 mL teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 160 °C for 6 h. Then, the solution was centrifuged at 7500 rpm for 30 min to remove large particles. The supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to obtain the desired CDs, which were stored at 4 °C for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TC and OTC based on W-CDs/Eu3+
ce:section.ce:para: 5 µL of W-CDs solution (10.6 mg·mL−1) was first mixed with Eu (NO3)3 solution. Then, 7.5 μL of TC/OTC solution (0.01 M) and 200 μL of Britton-Robinson buffer (BR buffer, pH 9.0) were added to the test tube. The final volume of the mixture was adjusted to 3 mL using ultrapure water. The ratio of FL intensity at F616/F467 was measured at the 370 nm excitation wavelength for the quantitative analysis of TC/OTC. The experimental conditions were optimized, including the concentration of Eu3+ (0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.25 mg·mL−1), pH (5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11) and volume of BR buffer (100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600 μL). In order to evaluate the selectivity of W-CDs/Eu3+, substances (25 µM) that may be present in actual water samples (other antibiotics, amino acids, and metal ions), were combined with the FL probe in place of the TC and OTC to determine the selectivity. To ensure the accuracy of the experiments, all experiments were repeated three times.

Detection of TC and OTC in aquaculture water was carried out by ratiometric FL probe. Aquaculture water samples were obtained from Deqing Lake and the Aquaculture Center of the College of Life Sciences, Huzhou University, China and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane.

5 µL of W-CDs solution (10.6 mg·mL−1) and Eu (NO3)3 solution were added into 200 µL BR buffer (pH 9), and then two actual water samples were added so that the total volume was made up to 3 mL. Different concentrations of TC and OTC (0, 5, 25 μM) standard solutions were added to the actual samples and their FL spectra were recorded under 370 nm excitation. All FL spectra were also carried out three times to ensure the accuracy of the experiments.

To achieve visual quantitative detection of TC, common quantitative filter paper was employed as a carrier and cut into uniformly sized square strips. The paper strips were immersed in W-CDs/Eu3+ (17.67 mg·L−1 W-CDs and 1 mg·mL−1 Eu3+) solution for 5 min and subsequently vacuum-dried at 40 °C for 30 min. To ensure complete loading of the W-CDs/Eu3+ solution onto the test strips, the soaking-drying cycle was repeated 4–5 times. Then, 20 μL of TC solutions at varying concentrations were dropped onto the paper strips. Under a 365 nm UV lamp (LUV-260SL UV transilluminator, 90 W, LUYOR, USA), distinct color variations were observed on the strips. Fluorescent images were captured using a smartphone (Honor 80Pro) camera, and the RGB values were extracted using color recognition software (Color Grab APP). The ratio of red channel value to blue channel value (R/B) was used as a parameter to establish a linear relationship between the R/B value and TC concentration. To minimize interference from external light sources and enhance experimental accuracy, FL images were captured at night. Furthermore, three parallel experiments were performed to reduce errors and improve the reliability of the results.

The TEM and HRTEM images, along with the size distribution of W-CDs, are presented in Fig. 1 A and B. The CDs exhibit monodisperse, quasi-spherical structures with uniform distribution and an average diameter of 6.59 nm. A distinct lattice structure with a spacing of 0.21 nm is observed in the HRTEM images, corresponding to the graphitic carbon (100) diffraction plane [37,38]. As illustrated in Fig. 1C, the elemental peaks of C, N and O can be seen in the full spectrum of XPS of W-CDs. Three peaks around 284.8, 285.2 and 287.9 e V are observed in the C 1 s XPS spectra in Fig. 1D, which are attributed to the C-C/C=C, C-N and C=O/C=N bonds [33] in W-CDs, respectively. Deconvolution of the high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectrum reveals two peaks at 398.4 eV and 398.7 eV, corresponding to pyridine-N and pyrrolic-N [39], respectively (Fig. 1E). The O 1 s XPS spectrum of W-CDs (Fig. 1F) exhibits two main peaks at 530.3 eV and 531.4 eV, corresponding to C=O and C-O-C, respectively. Furthermore, the atomic ratios of C, N, and O in W-CDs are 79.61 %, 8.13 %, 12.26 %, respectively. The FT-IR spectra further elucidate the chemical composition of W-CDs. As depicted in Fig. S1A, the broad absorption band centered at 3320 cm−1 is attributed to the O-H and N-H stretching vibration [40]. The absorption bands at 2975 cm−1 and 1631 cm−1 are attributed to C-H stretching [41–43] and the stretching vibration of the C=O [32,33,44,45] and the C=N [46] groups. The sharp peak at approximately 1360 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of the C-N bond [32,47]. The absorption peak at 1050 cm−1 indicates the vibration of C-O-C bonds [48,49]. Thus, CDs were successfully doped with N and contain various functional groups such as amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups [50]. These charged functional groups effectively prevented the aggregation of carbon dots through electrostatic repulsion. The negative zeta potential of −13.39 mV (data not shown) confirms the high stability of the CDs due to the strong electrostatic repulsion between particles. In addition, the high polarity of water enables it to strongly interact with the hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of the CDs, which are benefit to improve the water-solubility [51]. The XRD pattern of W-CDs (Fig. S1B) exhibits broad diffraction peaks at 21.6°, indicating partial graphitization. Compared to graphite (0.34 nm), the prepared W-CDs exhibit a larger interlayer spacing (0.41 nm for the (002) diffraction peak), which can be attributed to the incorporation of N-containing groups during synthesis [52,53]. Additionally, the amorphous nature of the W-CDs surface contributes to the broad width of the diffraction peak [54,55].

UV–vis absorption and FL spectra were employed to characterize the optical characteristics of W-CDs, as depicted in Fig. S1C. W-CDs exhibit two distinct absorption peaks at 283 nm and 342 nm, owing to the π-π transition of C=C [56] and the n-π* electronic transition of the C=O group [57], respectively. When the excitation wavelength changes from 320 nm to 390 nm, W-CDs exhibit excitation-independent emission (Fig. S2A), which may be due to their uniform surface states and narrow size distribution [58].

The influence of various factors on the optical stability of W-CDs was also investigated. First, the salt tolerance of W-CDs was investigated, as illustrated in Fig. S1D. The FL intensity remained almost unchanged when the NaCl concentration was increased from 0 to 1 M, indicating the potential applicability of these CDs in saline environments. The effect of time on the FL intensity of W-CDs was also examined. As shown in Fig. S1E, after storage at room temperature for 30 days, only a slight decrease in FL intensity was observed, demonstrating the excellent stability of the synthesized W-CDs. Next, the effect of UV irradiation time on the FL intensity of W-CDs was investigated (Fig. S1F). Under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation (90 W), the FL intensity was measured at 20-minute intervals. The FL intensity decreased by 7 % after 120 min, confirming the better photostability of W-CDs. In addition, using quinine sulfate as a reference, the quantum yield (QY) of W-CDs was calculated to be 16.9 % according to the following equation.
QY
x
=
QY
std
×
A
std
/
A x
×
I x
/
I
std
×
η x
/
η
std
2
Where I, A and η are the integral area of the FL peak, the integral area of the absorbance and the refractive index of the solvent, respectively. The subscripts “x” and “std” refer to W-CDs and quinine sulphate, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The feasibility of the W-CDs/Eu3+ ratiometric FL probe for TC/OTC monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: First, the feasibility of the W-CDs/Eu3+ ratiometric FL probe for TC/OTC detection was evaluated. As shown in Fig. S3C, the FL intensity of W-CDs/Eu3+ is almost the same as that of W-CDs, indicating that the addition of Eu3+ on the FL emission of W-CDs is negligible. The FL intensities of Eu3+ show gradually enhanced emission at 596 nm and 616 nm with increasing TC/OTC concentration in the absence of CDs (Fig. S3A and B). This phenomenon arises from the strong coordination of the β-diketonate structure in tetracycline with the Eu3+ ion, forming a stable tetracycline-Eu3+ complex [15]. However, the limited OTC/TC ligands make the majority of the Eu3+ sites being occupied by water molecules [15], leading to the FL quenching by the water molecules and lower sensitivity. As shown in Fig. S3C, when CDs, Eu3+ and TC/OTC coexist, the emission of W-CDs at 467 nm is quenched, while that of Eu3+ at 596 nm and 616 nm are significantly enhanced (greater than Eu3+/TC and Eu3+/OTC system) by efficiently eliminating the FL quenching by H2O molecules. Especially, the emission at 616 nm exhibits a more pronounced response, making it suitable as the FL signal of Eu3+ for TC/OTC detection. As a result, the W-CDs/Eu3+ system exhibits inverse changes in FL signals responses, which is essential for the development of a ratiometric FL probe based on the W-CDs/Eu3+ complex.

The ratiometric FL probe based on W-CDs/Eu3+ was designed to detect TC and OTC. With the addition of TC and OTC, the FL intensity of W-CDs was quenched, while that of Eu3+ was enhanced. The optimal excitation wavelength for W-CDs was 340 nm (Fig. S2A), while that for Eu3+ was 400 nm (Fig. S2B). Considering these excitation wavelengths together, 370 nm was chosen as the optimal excitation wavelength for the ratiometric FL probe.

The optimal conditions for the detection of TC and OTC were determined by calculating the FL intensity ratios of F616/F467, which mainly involved the concentrations of W-CDs and Eu3+, the pH value, and the amount of BR buffer. Due to the similar structure of TC and OTC, TC was selected as the model to optimize detection performance by calculating the FL intensity ratio of F616/F467. As shown in Fig. S4A and B, the optimal concentrations of W-CDs and Eu3+ were determined to be 17.67 mg·L−1 and 1 mg·mL−1, respectively. The optimal pH of the BR buffer was found to be 9 (Fig. S4C), with an optimal volume of 200 μL (Fig. S4D). The probe demonstrates optimal performance under alkaline conditions, which is attributed to the fact that the protonation process of the CDs in alkaline environments improves the coordination ability of the ring structure, thus enhancing the red-light emission of Eu3+ [59,60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric FL monitoring for TC and OTC
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized experimental conditions, the response performance of the FL probe toward TC and OTC was investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 , the ratiometric FL probe constructed from W-CDs/Eu3+ exhibited strong blue FL with negligible Eu3+ emission before the addition of TC and OTC. The ratios of F616/F467 gradually increased with increasing concentrations of TC/OTC, as illustrated in Fig. 2A and B. A good linear relationship was observed in Fig. 2C and D between F616/F467 versus the concentrations of TC/OTC (CTC/COTC) within the ranges of 0.0067–25 μM and 0.0067–21 μM, respectively. The linear equations are F616/F467 = 0.424CTC + 0.274 (R2 = 0.991), and F616/F467 = 0.453COTC + 0.299 (R2 = 0.997), respectively. The limits of detection (LOD, 3σ/k) for TC and OTC were calculated to be 2.96 nM and 2.85 nM, respectively. The ratiometric FL probe achieves lower LOD and wider linear ranges, which is lower and wider than most of reported literatures using CDs/Eu3+ probe [18,33,61,62] (Table S1). W-CDs/Eu3+ exhibit very low LOD, which is very close to the lowest LOD reported so far when using CD-Eu3+ as FL probe to monitor TC or OTC [63,64]. To our knowledge, it exhibits the lowest LOD among biomass-derived Eu3+-CDs system for monitoring TCs [16,35,36]. In addition, under a 365 nm UV lamp, the color change from blue to red is clearly observed (insets in Fig. 2C and D), allowing visual identification and semi-quantitative determination of OTC/TC with the naked eye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the W-CDs/Eu3+ hybrid FL probe for TCs monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the selectivity of the W-CDs/Eu3+ hybrid FL probe for TC/OTC monitoring, various interfering substances, including antibiotics (CTC, DOX, EM, AMC, GM, KAN, ENR, CIP, LOM, LVX, AZI, CPL,CLR, TAP, SM), metal ions (Fe3+, Cu2+, Hg+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Co2+), and amino acids (L-Asp, L-Arg, L-Ala, L-Cys, and L-Lys) were analyzed, with concentration of 25 μM. As demonstrated in Fig. 3 , only TCs with similar chemical structures and functional groups (TC, CTC, OTC, and DOX) exhibited significant changes in the F616/F467 ratio signal. Overall, the ratiometric FL probe based on W-CDs/Eu3+ demonstrates excellent selectivity for the detection of TC and OTC. The good selectivity originates from the specific recognition of TC/OTC with Eu3+ through AE and acquisition of ratio signals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of W-CDs/Eu3+ for TC and OTC detection
ce:section.ce:para:
To elucidate the quenching mechanism of the blue emission at 467 nm of W-CDs by TC/OTC. The UV–vis absorption spectra of TC/OTC and the excitation spectra of W-CDs were demonstrated in Fig. S5A, which reveals partial spectral overlap between them. This phenomenon indicates the presence of an IFE between TC/OTC and W-CDs. To further understand the interaction among W-CDs, Eu3+, and TC/OTC, the luminescence lifetimes of W-CDs, W-CDs-Eu3+, and W-CDs-Eu3+-TC/OTC at 467 nm were measured. As shown in Fig. S5B, the FL lifetimes of W-CDs, W-CDs + Eu3+, W-CDs + Eu3++TC and W-CDs + Eu3++OTC at 467 nm were 5.74, 5.66, 5.52 and 5.1 ns, respectively. Despite the addition of OTC and TC, the FL lifetimes of W-CDs remain almost unchanged, indicating that their FL quenching is primarily driven by static quenching. Then, the Stern-Volmer equation was employed to further explain the mechanism of FL quenching:
F 0
F
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
[ Q ]
Where F0 and F represent the FL intensities of W-CDs (I467) in the absence and presence of the quencher, respectively. Kq is the quenching rate constant for TC/OTC and
τ 0
is the FL emission excited state lifetime of W-CDs without TC/OTC. [Q] represents the concentration of TC/OTC. The calculated values of Kq were 1.29 × 1013 L mol−1 s−1 for TC and 1.61 × 1013 L mol−1 s−1 for OTC. Both of them are significantly higher than 2.0 × 1010 L mol−1 s−1, indicating the possibility of static quenching [46,65,66]. Therefore, the quenching of W-CDs by TC/OTC was primarily due to the IFE and the static quenching. In addition, TC and OTC effectively enhance the FL intensity at wavelength of 616 nm through specific chelation with Eu3+, functioning as an “antenna” [32]. This AE [67] facilitates ET to Eu3+. Compared to TC/OTC alone, the absorption peaks of W-CDs + Eu3++TC/OTC show significant red-shift and increased absorption intensity (Fig. S5A), indicating that chelation between TC/OTC and Eu3+ occurs [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted W-CDs/Eu3+ sensor for TC visualization detection
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, a simple, portable and low-cost sensing platform for the quantitative detection of TC was developed based on the W-CDs/Eu3+ ratiometric FL probe. A smartphone application (Color Grab) was employed as a FL color reading tool to convert color signals into color information (GBR values). In order to facilitate the detection, W-CDs/Eu3+ was immobilized on the surface of a common quantitative filter paper to create a test strip for TC monitoring. FL images were captured using a smartphone camera (HONOR 80Pro). As shown in Fig. S6, under 365 nm UV light, the test strip exhibited a gradual color change from blue to red with increasing TC concentration. The color ratio (R/B) had a good linear relationship with TC concentration in the range of 0.0067–25 μM, and the corresponding linear equation was R/B = 0.405CTC + 0.309 (R2 = 0.996). The slope of smartphone-assisted method is very close to the above ratiometric fluorometric method. This indicates that the smartphone-assisted sensing platform enables visual and quantitative detection of TC, offering a convenient and practical solution.

The W-CDs/Eu3+ −based ratiometric FL probe was used to detect TC and OTC in real water samples. As shown in Table S2 and S3, different concentrations of TC and OTC standard solutions were added to actual lake and aquatic water. The recovery rates of TC and OTC by the ratiometric FL measurement method ranged from 97.33 % to 102.6 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 3.33 %. The results of the proposed method in this work were in agreement with that measured by HPLC, validating the accuracy of the method for TC/OTC detection in real samples. In addition, the developed smartphone-assisted W-CDs/Eu3+-based ratiometric FL sensor was also applied to detect TC in lake water and aquatic water. As presented in Table 1 , the smartphone-based W-CDs/Eu3+ hybrid FL sensor achieved better recovery for TC (92 % to 105 %) and reasonable RSD of less 6.9 %. Also, the results of smartphone-assisted method were coincident with that measured by HPLC (Table 1), confirming the potential applicability of the method for TC/OTC detection in real samples. Overall, the detection results of TC and OTC in actual samples were satisfactory, demonstrating the practicality of this method.

W-CDs were prepared from biomass carrot (carbon source) and PEI (nitrogen source) using a hydrothermal method. By simply mixing W-CDs and Eu3+, the ratiometric FL probe (W-CDs/Eu3+) was constructed for the sensitive detection of TC and OTC. The blue FL of W-CDs was quenched, primarily due to the IFE and the static quenching. Moreover, owing to the dual response and reverse FL response, the FL probe can achieve ratiometric and visual detection of TC and OTC. When different concentrations of TC/OTC are added to this fluorescent probe, a clear FL color changes can be seen under a 365 nm UV lamp, allowing for visual identification and semi-quantitative determination of OTC/TC with the naked eye. Finally, a low-cost and portable smartphone-assisted visual readout was developed based on FL color changes, enabling its application in residual TC detection in aquaculture water. The advantages of this work include the following points. First, the precursor is biomass carrot, which is cheap and non-toxic. Second, the method exhibits very low detection limit. To our knowledge, W-CDs/Eu3+ exhibits the lowest LOD among biomass-derived Eu3+-CDs system for monitoring TCs. Third, this method demonstrates a wider linear range of nearly four orders of magnitude for the detection of TC/OTC. Fourth, the smartphone-assisted sensing platform enables both visual and quantitative detection of TC. All in all, the proposed FL probe holds significant potential for broad applications in TCs monitoring.

Zhaofeng Wang: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Validation. Hui Xu: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Lihua Wang: Funding acquisition. Xinxiang Zheng: Formal analysis. Tengzhi Zhao: Validation. Bowen Qu: Formal analysis. Hua Wang: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful to the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2023YFB3208200) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. na (No. 22074079) for support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113398.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
